---
title: "AI-usage-disclosure"
editor: visual
---

[**AI Tool Used:**]{.underline} Google Gemini V3

[**Prompt:**]{.underline} I want to add a 20m boundary extending from the coast inland and out into the water for my plot. Along the way, show me where the new code is used in relation to my original code.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[**Gemini Response**]{.underline}

To create a "zoomed-in" plot highlighting the 20-mile coastal zone, you can continue using the base R maps package while adjusting your xlim and ylim to focus on the Gulf and Atlantic coasts.

To visualize the 20-mile offshore zone, we can draw a "buffer" by slightly expanding the state lines using the st_buffer function from the sf package (which is the modern standard for spatial data in R). Updated Base R Code

```{r}
#| warning: false
library(maps)
library(sf)

# 1. Turn off the strict spherical engine to allow "imperfect" map data
sf_use_s2(FALSE) 

# 2. Re-create the states object and make it valid
states_sf <- st_as_sf(map("state", plot = FALSE, fill = TRUE))
states_sf <- st_make_valid(states_sf)

# 3. Now the union and buffer will work without the error
land_mass <- st_union(states_sf)
total_buffer <- st_buffer(land_mass, dist = 0.3)

# 4. Run your original commands
coastal_zone <- st_buffer(st_union(states_sf), dist = 0.3) 

# 5. (Optional) Turn s2 back on for other work
sf_use_s2(TRUE)

```

This code zooms in on the Southeast U.S. and adds a light-blue "shark zone" extending into the ocean.

```{r}
#| warning: false
library(sf)
library(maps)

# IMPORTANT: Fix for the "Edge crosses edge" error
sf_use_s2(FALSE) 

# 1. Setup the Map Data
states_sf <- st_as_sf(map("state", plot = FALSE, fill = TRUE))
states_sf <- st_make_valid(states_sf) # Repairs the 'broken' edges in the map

# 2. Create the 20-mile coastal "Shark Zone"
land_mass <- st_union(states_sf)
coastal_zone <- st_buffer(land_mass, dist = 0.3) 

# 3. Define the Zoom Area
zoom_lon <- c(-98, -75)
zoom_lat <- c(24, 38)

# 4. Generate the Plot
plot(NA, 
     xlim = zoom_lon, ylim = zoom_lat,
     xlab = "Longitude", ylab = "Latitude",
     asp = 1,
     main = "Coastal Shark-Lifting Hazard Zone (2021-2025)")

# Draw the 20-mile offshore buffer in light blue
plot(coastal_zone, col = adjustcolor("lightblue", alpha.f = 0.3), border = NA, add = TRUE)

# Re-add state lines for context
map("state", col = "grey60", add = TRUE)

# Add Shark Capture Locations

#Added this in during editing to make sure compiling worked well. This was not in the original AI response.
shark_survey <- read.csv("sharks.csv")
names(shark_survey)[names(shark_survey) == "Longitude.of.capture"] <- "cap.long"
names(shark_survey)[names(shark_survey) == "Latitude.of.Capture"] <- "cap.lat"

sharks <- shark_survey[shark_survey$TAXON %in% c("RHIZOPRIONODON TERRAENOVAE", "CARCHARHINUS PLUMBEUS", "CARCHARHINUS LIMBATUS", "CARCHARHINUS ACRONOTUS", "GALEOCERDO CUVIER", "MUSTELUS SINUSMEXICANUS", "CARCHARHINUS BREVIPINNA", "SPHYRNA LEWINI", "GINGLYMOSTOMA CIRRATUM"),]
sharks20 <- sharks[sharks$YEAR>=2021,] 
#End of Additions

points(sharks20$cap.long, sharks20$cap.lat,
       col = "darkblue", pch = 16, cex = 0.4)

# Add Tornado End Positions

#Added this in during editing to make sure compiling worked well. This was not in the original AI response.
storm2021 <- read.csv("storm2021.csv")
storm2022 <- read.csv("storm2022.csv")
storm2023 <- read.csv("storm2023.csv")
storm2024 <- read.csv("storm2024.csv")
storm2025 <- read.csv("storm2025.csv")
library(maps)
library(stringr)
tor21 <- storm2021[,c("STATE", "BEGIN_LON", "END_LON", "BEGIN_LAT", "END_LAT", "TOR_F_SCALE", "EVENT_TYPE", "EVENT_NARRATIVE")]
tor22 <- storm2022[,c("STATE", "BEGIN_LON", "END_LON", "BEGIN_LAT", "END_LAT", "TOR_F_SCALE", "EVENT_TYPE", "EVENT_NARRATIVE")]
tor23 <- storm2023[,c("STATE", "BEGIN_LON", "END_LON", "BEGIN_LAT", "END_LAT", "TOR_F_SCALE", "EVENT_TYPE", "EVENT_NARRATIVE")]
tor24 <- storm2024[,c("STATE", "BEGIN_LON", "END_LON", "BEGIN_LAT", "END_LAT", "TOR_F_SCALE", "EVENT_TYPE", "EVENT_NARRATIVE")]
tor25 <- storm2025[,c("STATE", "BEGIN_LON", "END_LON", "BEGIN_LAT", "END_LAT", "TOR_F_SCALE", "EVENT_TYPE", "EVENT_NARRATIVE")]
storm <- rbind(tor21, tor22, tor23, tor24, tor25)
tornado <- storm[storm$EVENT_TYPE == "Tornado",]
ec <- c("TEXAS", "LOUISIANA", "MISSISSIPPI", "ALABAMA", "FLORIDA", "GEORGIA", "SOUTH CAROLINA", "NORTH CAROLINA", "VIRGINIA", "DELAWARE", "PENNSYLVANIA", "NEW JERSEY", "NEW YORK")
ec.tornado <- tornado[tornado$STATE %in% ec,]
clean.tor <- ec.tornado[!is.na(ec.tornado$BEGIN_LON) & !is.na(ec.tornado$BEGIN_LAT) & !is.na(ec.tornado$TOR_F_SCALE), ]
ef.scale <- c("darkgreen", "yellow", "orange", "red", "maroon", "black")
strong.tor <- clean.tor[clean.tor$EF_SCALE>=3,]
#End of Additions

points(strong.tor$END_LON, strong.tor$END_LAT, 
       col = ef.scale[strong.tor$EF_SCALE + 1],
       pch = 17, cex = 1.2) 

# Add Legend
legend("bottomright", 
       legend = c("Shark Capture", "Tornado End (EF3-5)", "20mi Coastal Zone"), 
       col = c("darkblue", "red", "lightblue"),
       pch = c(16, 17, 15), 
       pt.cex = c(0.6, 1, 2),
       bg = "white", cex = 0.8)
```

To add a 20-mile border that extends inland as well, you just need to change how you subtract the shapes. Instead of cutting the land out of the buffer, you keep the full buffer as a "ribbon" that straddles the coastline. Here is the updated code to create a 40-mile total "Hazard Ribbon" (20 miles out + 20 miles in) and the zoomed-in plots:

```{r}
#| warning: false
# 1. Create the "Ribbon" (20 miles out AND 20 miles in)
# We take the boundary (lines) of the states and buffer THAT
sf_use_s2(FALSE)
state_lines <- st_cast(st_union(states_sf), "MULTILINESTRING")
hazard_ribbon <- st_buffer(state_lines, dist = 0.3) # 0.3 degrees (~20mi) each side

# 2. Zoom coordinates (Gulf & Atlantic focus)
zoom_x <- c(-98, -75) 
zoom_y <- c(24, 38)

# --- PLOT 1: STARTING POSITIONS ---
plot(NA, xlim = zoom_x, ylim = zoom_y, asp = 1,
     xlab = "Longitude", ylab = "Latitude",
     main = "Starting Position: 20mi Coastal Hazard Ribbon")

# Add the ribbon (20mi in/out)
plot(hazard_ribbon, col = adjustcolor("lightblue", alpha.f = 0.4), border = NA, add = TRUE)
map("state", col = "grey40", add = TRUE)

# Points
points(sharks20$cap.long, sharks20$cap.lat, col = "darkblue", pch = 16, cex = 0.4)
points(strong.tor$BEGIN_LON, strong.tor$BEGIN_LAT, col = ef.scale[strong.tor$EF_SCALE+1], pch = 16)

# --- PLOT 2: ENDING POSITIONS ---
plot(NA, xlim = zoom_x, ylim = zoom_y, asp = 1,
     xlab = "Longitude", ylab = "Latitude",
     main = "Ending Position: 20mi Coastal Hazard Ribbon")

# Add the ribbon
plot(hazard_ribbon, col = adjustcolor("lightblue", alpha.f = 0.4), border = NA, add = TRUE)
map("state", col = "grey40", add = TRUE)

# Shark & Tornado Data
points(sharks20$cap.long, sharks20$cap.lat, col = "darkblue", pch = 16, cex = 0.4)
segments(x0=strong.tor$BEGIN_LON, y0=strong.tor$BEGIN_LAT, 
         x1=strong.tor$END_LON, y1=strong.tor$END_LAT, col="black", lwd=1)
points(strong.tor$END_LON, strong.tor$END_LAT, col = ef.scale[strong.tor$EF_SCALE+1], pch = 16)

legend("bottomright", legend = c("Shark Capture", "EF3", "EF4", "EF5", "20mi Ribbon"), 
       col = c("darkblue", ef.scale[4:6], "lightblue"), pch = c(16, 16, 16, 16, 15), 
       bg = "white", cex = 0.7)

```

Differences and Adjustments Made:

```{r}
#| warning: false
#| layout-ncol: 2
#| column: page
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 6
#| fig-subcap: 
#|   - "Starting Tornado Positions from 2021-2025"
#|   - "Ending Tornado Positions from 2021-2025 (Line-Segments show Tornado Path)"

#I reorganized these first lines a bit to group the sf portion together.
strong.tor <- clean.tor[clean.tor$EF_SCALE>=3,]

#I changed some of the names that I will be using multiple times to better fit with my naming convention.
library(sf)
sf_use_s2(FALSE)
states_sf <- st_as_sf(map("state", plot=FALSE, fill=TRUE))
states_sf <- st_make_valid(states_sf)
state.lines <- st_cast(st_union(states_sf), "MULTILINESTRING")
sharknado.zone <- st_buffer(state.lines, dist=0.3)

#My own original code. I didn't change the x and y limits to the ones Gemini said to because I thought the way they were already presenting my plots worked fine.
plot(strong.tor$BEGIN_LON, strong.tor$BEGIN_LAT, type="n", 
     xlab="Longitude",
     xlim=c(-100, -60),
     ylab="Latitude",
     ylim=c(20, 50),
     asp=1,
     main="Starting Position of Potnential Shark-Lifting U.S. Tornados from 2021-2025")
map("state", col="grey", fill=FALSE, add=TRUE)
points(strong.tor$BEGIN_LON, strong.tor$BEGIN_LAT, 
       col=ef.scale[strong.tor$EF_SCALE+1], 
       pch=16)
points(sharks20$cap.long, sharks20$cap.lat,
       col="lightblue", 
       pch=16,
       cex=0.5)
legend("bottomright", 
       legend = c("EF3", "EF4", "EF5"), 
       col=ef.scale[4:6],
       pch=16, 
       cex=0.8, 
       title="Starting EF Rating")
#I changed the name to better reflect my own naming conventions, and I changed the color to make the final plot more visually striking.
plot(sharknado.zone, col=adjustcolor("orange", alpha.f=0.4), border=NA, add=TRUE)

#My own original code. I didn't change the x and y limits because I thought the way they were already presenting my plots worked fine.
plot(strong.tor$END_LON, strong.tor$END_LAT, type="n", 
     xlab="Longitude",
     xlim=c(-100, -60),
     ylab="Latitude",
     ylim=c(20, 50),
     asp=1,
     main="Ending Position of Potential Shark-Lifting U.S. Tornados from 2021-2025")
segments(x0=strong.tor$BEGIN_LON,
         y0=strong.tor$BEGIN_LAT, 
         x1=strong.tor$END_LON,
         y1=strong.tor$END_LAT, 
         col=adjustcolor("black", alpha.f=1.5), lwd=1)
points(strong.tor$BEGIN_LON, strong.tor$BEGIN_LAT, 
       col=adjustcolor("darkgrey", alpha.f=1), 
       pch=16, 
       cex=0.75)
points(strong.tor$END_LON, strong.tor$END_LAT, 
       col=ef.scale[strong.tor$EF_SCALE+1],
       pch=16)
points(sharks20$cap.long, sharks20$cap.lat,
       col="lightblue", 
       pch=16,
       cex=0.5)
#I changed the name to better reflect my own naming conventions, and I changed the color to make the final plot more visually striking.
plot(sharknado.zone, col=adjustcolor("orange", alpha.f=0.4), border=NA, add=TRUE)
map("state", col="grey", fill=FALSE, add=TRUE)
legend("bottomright", 
       legend=c("EF3", "EF4", "EF5"), 
       col=ef.scale[4:6],
       pch=16, 
       cex=0.8, 
       title="Ending EF Rating")

```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[**AI Tool Used:**]{.underline} Google Gemini V3

[**Prompt:**]{.underline} I want to count the number of data points within the buffer zone. How can I do that. Please show me the code you use and then show how I can integrate it with the rest of my current code so that it works when I compile it.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[**Gemini Response**]{.underline}

To count the number of dots (tornado or shark points) within your buffer, you need to convert your coordinate data into sf objects and then use st_intersects(). Here is how you can do it for both your tornadoes and sharks:

1.  Convert points to sf objects Since your data currently exists as data frames with longitude and latitude, convert them to spatial objects first:

```{r}
#| warning: false
# Convert Tornadoes to sf
strong_tor_sf <- st_as_sf(strong.tor, 
                          coords = c("BEGIN_LON", "BEGIN_LAT"), 
                          crs = st_crs(states_sf))

# Convert Sharks to sf
sharks_sf <- st_as_sf(sharks20, 
                      coords = c("cap.long", "cap.lat"), 
                      crs = st_crs(states_sf))
```

2.  Count the intersections Use st_intersects() to find which points fall inside the buffer. This returns a list; checking the length of the unlisted result gives you the total count.

To get your count to reflect unique locations (or unique sharks, if there is a Tag ID column), you need to filter for distinct rows before running the buffer intersection. The Fixed Code for your Count:

Add this block at the very end of your script. It uses unique() on the coordinates to ensure each capture location is only counted once.

```{r}
#| warning: false
# 1. Convert to SF, but keep only UNIQUE capture locations
# This collapses your 2,831 rows back down to unique spatial points
sharks_sf <- st_as_sf(sharks20, 
                      coords=c("cap.long", "cap.lat"), 
                      crs=st_crs(states_sf))

# Use unique() to remove duplicate coordinate rows
sharks_unique_sf <- unique(sharks_sf)

# 2. Run the intersection on the UNIQUE hazard ribbon locations
# This ensures a shark at one spot isn't counted multiple times
sharks.matrix <- st_intersects(sharks_unique_sf, hazard_ribbon, sparse = FALSE)

# 3. Calculate the count
# rowSums > 0 checks if that specific location falls in ANY buffer segment
unique_shark_count <- sum(rowSums(sharks.matrix) > 0)

paste("Number of Unique Shark Locations in Buffer:", unique_shark_count)
```

Here is the updated code for the tornadoes:

```{r}
#| warning: false
# 1. Convert to SF and keep only UNIQUE tornado start locations
# This prevents counting the same tornado record multiple times
sf_strong.tor <- st_as_sf(strong.tor, 
                          coords=c("BEGIN_LON", "BEGIN_LAT"), 
                          crs=st_crs(states_sf))

# Remove any duplicate rows if they exist in your data
tor_unique.sf <- unique(sf_strong.tor)

# 2. Directly filter the tornados that intersect the zone
# [ , ] syntax filters rows based on the spatial predicate
tor_in_buffer <- tor_unique.sf[st_intersects(tor_unique.sf, sharknado.zone, sparse = FALSE), ]

# 3. Count the rows of the resulting object
unique_tor_count <- nrow(tor_in_buffer)

# Handle the case where no tornados are found (returns 0 instead of NULL)
if(is.null(unique_tor_count)) unique_tor_count <- 0

paste("Number of Unique Tornados in Buffer:", unique_tor_count)
```

Why this works: st_as_sf: Turns your table into a spatial object the sf package understands. crs: It is vital that your points use the same Coordinate Reference System as your states_sf map. sparse = FALSE: Returns a simple True/False (logical) vector. Summing a logical vector counts every TRUE as 1.

Differences Made:

```{r}
#| warning: false

#Most of the changes made here were to the names to better fit my own naming conventions so I could understand the inputs and outputs better.
sharks.sf <- st_as_sf(sharks20, 
                      coords=c("cap.long", "cap.lat"), 
                      crs=st_crs(states_sf))
unique.sharks.sf <- unique(sharks.sf)
sharks_in_zone <- st_filter(unique.sharks.sf, sharknado.zone)
unique.shark.count <- nrow(sharks_in_zone)
paste("Number of Sharks in Sharknado Buffer Zone:", unique.shark.count)

#Note: This code will produce the incorrect answer that is not the one seen in the actual results section. Gemini and Quarto were having major issues between each other when compiling, and as a result I just needed to change the code to the minimum level where it would compile, even at the expense of some of the accuracy between the results and this changed code portion. This code is used as a foundation for the actual results, but because the dependencies are not the exact ones used in the results, the code breaks down a bit. The goal of this disclosure is to show you the foundation, even if the code fails here.

strong.tor_clean <- strong.tor[!is.na(strong.tor$BEGIN_LON) & !is.na(strong.tor$BEGIN_LAT),]
sf_strong.tor <- st_as_sf(strong.tor_clean, 
                          coords=c("BEGIN_LON", "BEGIN_LAT"), 
                          crs=st_crs(states_sf))
unique.tor.sf <- unique(sf_strong.tor)
tor_in_zone <- st_filter(unique.tor.sf, sharknado.zone)
unique.tor.count <- nrow(tor_in_zone)
paste("Number of Tornados in Sharknado Buffer Zone:", unique.tor.count)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[**AI Tool Used:**]{.underline} Google Gemini V3

[**Prompt:**]{.underline} I want to do a nearest neighbor classification for sharks and tornados within the buffer zone. Use the knn function in library(class). make a table where each row corresponds to each tornado in the buffer zone.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[**Gemini Response**]{.underline}

```{r}
#| warning: false

library(class)
library(sf)

#Added in Extra code to make the Gemini code work.#
sharks.sf <- st_as_sf(sharks20, 
                      coords=c("cap.long", "cap.lat"), 
                      crs=st_crs(states_sf))
unqiue.sharks.sf <- unique(sharks.sf)

# 1. Intersection (sparse=FALSE returns a 1-column matrix)
sharks.matrix <- st_intersects(unqiue.sharks.sf, sharknado.zone, sparse=FALSE)

# FIX: Use sum() on the logical matrix directly (No rowSums)
unique.shark.count <- sum(sharks.matrix)
paste("Number of Sharks in Buffer Zone:", unique.shark.count)

strong.tor_clean <- strong.tor[!is.na(strong.tor$BEGIN_LON) & !is.na(strong.tor$BEGIN_LAT),]
sf_strong.tor <- st_as_sf(strong.tor_clean, 
                          coords=c("BEGIN_LON", "BEGIN_LAT"), 
                          crs=st_crs(states_sf))

# FIX: renamed this to unique.tor.sf to avoid overwriting your shark data
unique.tor.sf <- unique(sf_strong.tor)
tor.matrix <- st_intersects(unique.tor.sf, sharknado.zone, sparse=FALSE)

# FIX: Use sum() directly
unique.tor.count <- sum(tor.matrix)
paste("Number of Tornados in Buffer Zone:", unique.tor.count)

# --- 1. Filter points in the buffer (Safe Method) ---
# Using [,1] ensures we treat the matrix as a simple TRUE/FALSE list
sharks_in_buffer <- unqiue.sharks.sf[sharks.matrix[, 1], ]
torns_in_buffer  <- unique.tor.sf[tor.matrix[, 1], ]

# --- 2. Extract Coordinates ---
train_shark <- st_coordinates(sharks_in_buffer)
train_empty <- st_coordinates(st_sample(sharknado.zone, size = 2675, exact = TRUE))
test_coords <- st_coordinates(torns_in_buffer)

# --- 3. Combine training data ---
train_coords <- rbind(train_shark, train_empty)

# --- 4. Create labels ---
n_shark <- nrow(train_shark)
n_empty <- nrow(train_empty)
train_labels <- c(rep("Shark_Present", n_shark), rep("No_Shark", n_empty))

# --- 5. Run kNN and build report ---
library(class)
knn_prediction <- knn(train = train_coords, 
                      test  = test_coords, 
                      cl    = train_labels, 
                      k     = 5, 
                      prob  = TRUE)

knn_report <- data.frame(
  Tornado_ID     = seq_along(knn_prediction),
  Classification = knn_prediction,
  Confidence     = attr(knn_prediction, "prob")
)
```

Differences Made:

```{r}

#Most of the changes made here were to the names to better fit my own naming conventions so I could understand the inputs and outputs better.
sharks.zone <- unqiue.sharks.sf[sharks.matrix[, 1], ]
tor.zone <- sf_strong.tor[tor.matrix[, 1], ]
ocean.zone <- st_sample(sharknado.zone, size = 2675)

shark.loc <- st_coordinates(sharks.zone)
ocean.loc <- st_coordinates(ocean.zone)
train.data <- rbind(shark.loc, ocean.loc)
test.data  <- st_coordinates(tor.zone)

table.labels <- c(rep("Shark_Present", nrow(shark.loc)), 
                  rep("No_Sharks_Present", nrow(ocean.loc)))

#Added Code adapted from Professor Bilder's 494 Class. This function creates a classification table that measures accuracy rates.#
summarize.class <- function(original, classify) {
  class.table <- table(original, classify)
  numb <- rowSums(class.table)
  prop <- round(class.table/numb,4)
  overall <- round(sum(diag(class.table))/sum(class.table),4)
  list(class.table = class.table, prop = prop, overall.correct = overall)
}

set.seed(349)
save.results.cv <- matrix(data = NA, nrow = 10, ncol = 4)

for (K in 1:10) {
  NNC.cv <- knn.cv(train = train.data, cl = table.labels, k = K)
  NNC.cv.accuracy <- summarize.class(original = table.labels, classify = NNC.cv)
  save.results.cv[K,] <- c(K, 
                           NNC.cv.accuracy$prop[1,1], 
                           NNC.cv.accuracy$prop[2,2], 
                           NNC.cv.accuracy$overall.correct)
}
#Altered the value of k from k=5 to k=1 based on the Results#
knn.pred <- knn(train = train.data, 
                test  = test.data, 
                cl    = table.labels, 
                k     = 1, 
                prob  = TRUE)

#This code is different from the final version I use for my actual project, as this page wouldn't compile unless it was self contained. Please refer to the draft-results section to see the final edited version.
n_results <- length(knn.pred)
conf_vals <- attr(knn.pred, "prob")
if (is.null(conf_vals) || length(conf_vals) == 0) {
  conf_vals <- rep(1, n_results)
}
knn.table <- data.frame(
  Tornado_Number = seq_len(n_results),
  Classification = as.character(knn.pred),
  Confidence     = as.numeric(conf_vals))
knitr::kable(knn.table)

```
